finding.summary: Clock divider registers are readable via APB without any access guard: the always_comb readback block returns r_clkdiv0/1/2 unconditionally regardless of PSEL, PENABLE, or any privilege/lock signal, exposing sensitive clock configuration state to any bus master.

evidence.description: r_clkdiv0, r_clkdiv1, and r_clkdiv2 hold the programmed clock divider values. These control system clock frequencies and are security-sensitive configuration registers.

evidence.description: The always_comb block at lines 85-97 performs PRDATA readback using only s_apb_addr as a selector, with no check on PSEL, PENABLE, or any privilege/lock signal. Any bus transaction (or even combinational glitch on PADDR) causes the internal register values to appear on PRDATA without authentication or access control.

evidence.description: PREADY is hardwired to 1'b1 (line 103) and PSLVERR to 1'b0 (line 104), meaning the peripheral always acknowledges every transaction immediately and never signals an error, preventing any upstream error-based access denial mechanism from functioning.

evidence.description: Because PRDATA is driven combinatorially from r_clkdiv0/1/2 without any access gate, any APB master (including untrusted software or a compromised peripheral) can read out current clock divider settings, potentially enabling clock-based side-channel attacks or aiding in further privilege escalation by revealing system configuration.
evidence.supports_claim: Unguarded readback of clock configuration registers constitutes a sensitive information disclosure vulnerability.

finding.reasoning_summary: The write path to the clock divider registers is protected by the standard APB handshake (PSEL && PENABLE && PWRITE), but the read path in the always_comb block at lines 85-97 has no equivalent guard. PRDATA is assigned directly from the internal registers based solely on the address, with no check that a valid read transaction is in progress (no PSEL/PENABLE/~PWRITE qualification). Additionally, PREADY is permanently 1 and PSLVERR permanently 0, so the peripheral can never refuse or flag any access. This asymmetry means clock divider values can be read by any bus master at any time without privilege checks.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or malicious APB bus master can read the current clock divider configuration without any authorization check. This discloses sensitive system configuration, and combined with the ability to write (subject only to standard APB signaling, not privilege), enables unauthorized modification and readback of clock control registers that govern system-wide timing.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: No higher-level bus firewall or APB interconnect access-control wrapper is visible in this chain context. If such a wrapper exists upstream and restricts which masters can reach this peripheral, the impact would be reduced. However, within the module itself, the absence of a read guard is clear and unconditional.

finding.reasoning_summary: The apb_soc_ctrl module controls highly privileged SoC functions including processor boot address, fetch enable, cluster power/reset/bypass, and JTAG output register. The spim1 or equivalent peripheral ready signal (if N_PERIPHS=8) is missing from the concatenation, causing a width mismatch or an implicit zero-padding of one bit position. This means one peripheral's ready acknowledgment is silently dropped or replaced with a constant 0, preventing that peripheral from ever completing a configuration transaction.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0001 declares eight distinct per-peripheral ready signals (spim0, sdio, i2c0, i2c1, uart, i2s, cam) plus s_periph_ready as an N_PERIPHS-wide array. If N_PERIPHS is 8 or greater, SystemVerilog will zero-extend the 7-bit concatenation to fill the remaining bit(s), meaning one or more peripheral slots will always report ready=0. This is a structural functional bug with security relevance: a peripheral whose ready is permanently 0 will stall the uDMA configuration bus, potentially causing denial-of-service or allowing unintended state where configuration writes appear to hang and could be retried to unintended peripherals.
finding.security_impact: One peripheral in the uDMA subsystem will permanently report not-ready on the configuration bus due to the missing ready signal in the concatenation. This can cause denial-of-service for that peripheral's configuration path and may allow uDMA configuration transactions to be misdirected or stalled, undermining access control to peripheral registers.
